text: At the edge of a hamlet, a carved waystone bears the old spiral glyph — Cinder-Seeker work, kept clean by someone who understood what it meant.

The fire bracket is still bolted to the rock, angled for a south-facing signal. Below it, scratched with a knife point: a name and the glyph for 'road-keeper.' Someone maintained this post long after the road fell silent.

Above the signal ridge, a stone shelter sits in the lee of the mountain. Waxed signal cord hangs in coils from wooden pegs — still wound, still usable. Beside it: a log book with one final entry in the keeper's hand. 'Cache sealed. Road complete. Come quickly.'

The wayhouse is mostly rubble, but the keeper's sealed alcove is untouched — built into the thickest wall, exactly as the log described. Inside: a fire lens on an iron chain. The glass is clouded from old heat, but it still focuses light into a point.

Below the final waystone, a stone niche sealed with iron. Inside, wrapped in signal cloth: the Ember Compass — a tool that tilts toward warmth instead of north. The road-keeper's last errand. You complete it.
A merchant's pack lies open on a broken counter in an abandoned trading post. Inside: a route map with one edge dipped in brine and the word 'Covenant' burned into the leather.
The salt shelf is cracked by old flood lines. At its center: a boundary stone with two names carved on opposing faces. One has been deliberately scratched out with something heavy.

The tower walls are charred at hip-height — fire set from inside, not out. Under a pried floorboard: a sealed letter addressed to both names on the boundary stone. It has been read and carefully re-sealed.

A sealed storeroom, long abandoned. Inside: provisions wrapped in waxed cloth, untouched — a gesture of compact left by someone who never came back to claim an answer. A note reads: 'Left in good faith for whoever comes.'

Under a flagstone marked with both names — one scratched out, one intact — a folded document wrapped in oilskin. The Salt Covenant: names, terms, and a final clause describing a sealed cache hidden beneath 'the first arch in the southern ruins.'

Under the first arch: a sealed chest. The trade goods it once held are long gone, but the Deep Pack used to carry them is intact — every seam stitched twice, impossible depth, balanced for a long road. Someone made this for a crossing that never happened.
A dead cartographer's notebook lies open on a ruined desk. The last entry: 'Three waypoints remaining. Pack sealed at the crossing. Do not wait for me.'
Three stones, still standing. Lodged beneath the middle one: a waxed tube holding the cartographer's partial map. The next mark is circled in red chalk and labeled 'echo valley.'

The echo valley is exactly as described — your footsteps return to you twice, slightly wrong. A second waxed tube is wedged into a wall crack. Inside: a map correction and a note. 'Pack is heavy. Left half at the crossing. The spikes are worth the weight.'

A flagstone marked with a compass rose. Below it: half a cartographer's pack. Food long spoiled, but tools intact — including a set of Glass Spikes wrapped in leather, labeled in the same hand: 'For the final climb.'

choices.label: Search the pack for anything edible

text: At the summit: a brass sight-ring bolted to the rock, angled west. You look through it. The cartographer was right — there is a clear corridor through the ash, still open. You pocket the Glass Spikes and fix the heading in your mind.
A wall frieze shows a warden in full mail standing at a sealed gate. The inscription below reads: 'The seal held longer than they thought.' The second line has been burned away.

choices.label: Knock a stone from the frieze

text: The gate lintel bears the seal glyph from the frieze. Carved into the wall beside it, a hollow barely big enough for a fist. Inside: a folded order on stiff paper. 'Retreat to the wayhouse. Do not burn the logs. The mail holds.'

Long abandoned, but the walls hold. A cold fire ring and a mostly-burned log book in the corner. The last readable line: 'I go now to the hollow place. The mail must not be lost. Look above the first arch.'

Wind-still and cool, the hollow is used for old rites — ash circles, carved stones, a fire ring built from memory rather than habit. In a stone depression: ash, two ritual pins, and a folded note. 'Look above the first arch when the light holds.'

Above the arch, in a stone corbel where morning light reaches: a rolled bundle wrapped in warded cloth. The Warded Mail — rings etched with old sigils, not a single one burned. The warden's seal held after all.
A shrine post at the forest edge holds a single knotted thread — one end burned, one end tucked into a crack in the post. A tile etched with the fire-keeper's glyph lies at its base.

The carved stump altar sits beside a cold brook. A second thread is tied to its base — longer than the first, ending in three loops. Three more places to find. The brook is the coldest water you have touched in days.

Ancient root hollows ring a clearing of collapsed light. In the largest: a fire-keeper's tin sealed with pitch. Inside, the third counting knot and a charcoal note — 'From here, the heat cannot follow.' It was optimistic. It was also kind.

The festival hearth ring is cold and deep with ash. The fourth knot is wedged between the hearthstones — the longest yet, each loop a different thickness. The keeper ran out of time on this one. The knots are still even.

The old road is half-buried in ash and brine. A route marker leans at an angle, wrapped in a familiar waxed thread. The knot it holds is different from the others — not a counting knot. It means 'safe.' The keeper left it for anyone who came this way.

The hollow is wind-still between two rock faces, exactly as described. In a niche at the back: a wrapped bundle. The fire-keeper's cord, complete — every knot intact. Each knot is a day of walking not yet lost.

choices.label: Tie the cord to your pack
